story:
Matt’s best friend Randy bursts in, all grin and dimples, and says he’s going to propose—will Matt design the ring? Propose to who? Angie, of course. Matt’s stomach drops. He introduced them two months ago, right after he’d finally worked up the nerve to ask Angie out himself. He still thinks of her as an angel: turquoise eyes like summer sky, dark hair that shimmers, a soothing laugh that softened his shyness. On their second date—ocean breeze, salmon-blush sunset, string lights—they bumped into Randy. Banter flew. Matt vanished in plain sight as he watched love-at-first-sight happen to the two people he didn’t want to lose.

He tells himself to be a good friend. Out loud: Congratulations. Inside: You asshole. But he says yes to the ring. Not for Randy—for Angie. He sketches a white-gold band scalloped because it’s feminine, a flower-shaped mount cupping the diamond, a matching wedding band that locks in place. Randy is all praise. “You’re very talented, bro.”

The restaurant fills with family and friends. Angie, in aqua and white, glows; Randy, in an aqua shirt, looks like fate is color-coordinating them. Matt feels himself redden when Angie hugs him, voice husky and kind. Then the glass tap, the kneel, the yes. The room erupts. “The ring is beautiful,” Angie murmurs. “Matt designed it,” Randy says proudly. Matt lifts a thumb, trying not to drown.

“Angie, there’s more,” Randy adds, pulling a jeweler’s loupe. “Look at the diamond’s girdle,” he says cheerfully—the place where the top and bottom meet. Guests chuckle. Matt does not. Horror strikes: he didn’t set Randy’s diamond. He set his own, a two-carat he’d secretly bought, laser-engraved with a message no one should see: MATT LOVES YOU MORE! He thought it was a hidden gift, a private truth. He never dreamed Randy would think to show her the inscription.

Angie peers. Silence stretches. Her face flushes. “Is this a joke?” she whispers. Matt bolts, chest burning. Randy finds him, slams the ring into his hand. “FIX IT.”

That night in the studio, Matt switches the stones, hands shaking while Randy glares. It feels like the end of a friendship. Months pass. Matt apologizes again and again; Angie, true to nature, helps them mend the tear.

But Matt can’t stitch his own heart. He tosses the engraved diamond into a drawer, then decides to move to New York for a fresh start. Before he goes, he retrieves the stone and hands it to a distant cousin, Benny, for “safekeeping.” He tells Angie and Randy to get the ring cleaned and the prongs checked yearly, and, oh, here’s a reliable jeweler—Benny. Unbeknownst to them, Benny’s scruples are soft. Unbeknownst to them, Matt is tempted to bribe him later to swap the stone back so Angie will wear his diamond in secret. It’s a desperate, manipulative plan—wrong, bound to hurt people—but pain makes him reckless.

At the airport, Randy and Angie wave. Matt is giddy, too giddy. Hasta la vista! he shouts, leaping like a man who just escaped or just made a mistake he’ll have to live with. Angie and Randy exchange a puzzled look. Matt’s joy seems…odd. The ring still gleams. So does the truth, hidden at the heart of a stone.
